We call it `calls` because "parallel" can be misleading: tools are not executed in parallel unless RubyLLM is configured to run them concurrently. `calls` describes the actual behavior directly: `:many` means multiple tool calls in one assistant response, `:one` means one tool call in one assistant response.
./docs/_core_features/tools.md:366:If `calls` is not provided, RubyLLM uses provider/model defaults, which are usually equivalent to `calls: :many`.
./docs/_core_features/tools.md:368:> Tool choice and call-count controls are provider/model dependent.

./docs/_core_features/citations.md:5:description: Get verifiable answers with normalized citations pointing at documents and web sources, on every provider that supports them
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:32:* How citations map to each provider's native format
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:37:Citations link spans of a model's answer back to the source material that supports them — a document you attached, or a web page found through search or grounding. They make answers verifiable and reduce hallucinations, and providers parse them server-side so cited quotes are guaranteed to exist in the source.
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:39:Every provider returns citations in a different shape. RubyLLM normalizes all of them into `RubyLLM::Citation` objects on `response.citations`, so your rendering code never branches on provider.
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:70:Document citations are currently supported by Anthropic. RubyLLM checks the [model registry]({% link _advanced/models.md %}) and logs a warning when you request citations from a model that can't return them. Citations from search and grounding are always parsed regardless (see below).
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:100:On Anthropic these become native citable search result blocks. Other providers receive the same results as plain text, so your tools stay provider-agnostic.
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:104:When a provider searches the web, RubyLLM parses the resulting citations automatically — no `with_citations` needed. Enable search the way each provider expects:
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:108:response = RubyLLM.chat(model: 'sonar', provider: :perplexity)
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:111:# Gemini with Google Search grounding
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:116:# OpenAI with web search
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:127:Each citation exposes a normalized set of fields. Fields a provider doesn't report are `nil`.
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:177:What arrives mid-stream varies by provider. Anthropic streams each citation (with `cited_text`) as the cited sentence completes, but without response positions; OpenAI sends its `url_citation` annotations in one chunk near the end of the stream, with positions but no quoted snippet. The finished message is always the complete picture: RubyLLM resolves `text` from `start_index`/`end_index` against the full content where positions are available.
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:180:## ActiveRecord Integration
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:216:## Provider Notes
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:218:- **Anthropic** returns the richest citations: quoted snippets, document titles, exact response spans, and page numbers for PDFs. Citations and structured output (`with_schema`) cannot be combined.
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:219:- **OpenAI** (and Azure/OpenRouter) return `url_citation` annotations from web search, with response spans.
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:220:- **Gemini / Vertex AI** return grounding metadata when the `google_search` tool is enabled. RubyLLM converts grounding byte offsets to character offsets for you.
./docs/_core_features/citations.md:229:* [Rails Integration]({% link _advanced/rails.md %})

./docs/_core_features/image-generation.md:86:Image costs use provider usage data plus pricing from the model registry. For models that report separate text and image input token details, RubyLLM applies the right pricing bucket to each part and returns the combined value as `image.cost.input`.

./docs/_core_features/chat.md:5:description: Learn how to have conversations with AI models, work with different providers, and handle multi-modal inputs
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:26:* How to start and continue conversations with AI models
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:27:* How to select and work with different models and providers
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:37:When you want to interact with an AI model, you create a chat instance. The simplest approach uses `RubyLLM.chat`, which creates a new conversation with your configured default model.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:43:response = chat.ask "Explain the concept of 'Convention over Configuration' in Rails."
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:47:# => "Convention over Configuration (CoC) is a core principle of Ruby on Rails..."
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:56:The `ask` method adds your message to the conversation history with the `:user` role, sends the entire conversation history to the AI provider, and returns a `RubyLLM::Message` object containing the assistant's response.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:62:One of the key features of chat-based AI models is their ability to maintain context across multiple exchanges. The `Chat` object automatically manages this conversation history for you.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:74:# => [USER] Explain the concept of 'Convention over Configuration' in Rails.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:75:# => [ASSISTANT] Convention over Configuration (CoC) is a core principle...
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:80:Each time you call `ask`, RubyLLM sends the entire conversation history to the AI provider. This allows the model to understand the full context of your conversation, enabling natural follow-up questions and maintaining coherent dialogue.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:107:System prompts are added to the conversation as messages with the `:system` role and are sent with every request to the AI provider. This ensures the model always considers your instructions when generating responses.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:109:> When using the [Rails Integration]({% link _advanced/rails.md %}), system messages are persisted in your database along with user and assistant messages, maintaining the full conversation context.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:114:RubyLLM supports over 600 models from various providers. While `RubyLLM.chat` uses your configured default model, you can specify different models:
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:118:chat_claude = RubyLLM.chat(model: '{{ site.models.anthropic_current }}')
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:119:chat_gemini = RubyLLM.chat(model: '{{ site.models.gemini_current_latest }}')
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:122:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: '{{ site.models.default_chat }}')
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:125:chat.with_model('{{ site.models.anthropic_latest }}')
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:129:For detailed information about model selection, capabilities, aliases, and working with custom models, see the [Working with Models Guide]({% link _advanced/models.md %}).
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:133:Many modern AI models can process multiple types of input beyond just text. RubyLLM provides a unified interface for working with images, audio, documents, and other file types through the `with:` parameter.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:137:Vision-capable models can analyze images, answer questions about visual content, and even compare multiple images.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:141:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: '{{ site.models.openai_vision }}')
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:158:You can also analyze video files or URLs with video-capable models. RubyLLM will automatically detect video files and handle them appropriately.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:175:> Supported video formats include .mp4, .mov, .avi, .webm, and others (provider-dependent).
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:177:> Only Google Gemini and VertexAI models currently support video input.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:179:> Large video files may be subject to size or duration limits imposed by the provider.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:182:RubyLLM automatically handles image encoding and formatting for each provider's API. Local images are read and encoded as needed, while URLs are passed directly when supported by the provider.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:186:Audio-capable models can transcribe speech, analyze audio content, and answer questions about what they hear. Currently, models like `{{ site.models.openai_audio }}` and Google's `gemini-2.5` series of models support audio input.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:189:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: '{{ site.models.openai_audio }}') # Use an audio-capable model
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:199:# Gemini example
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:207:You can provide text files directly to models for analysis, summarization, or question answering. This works with any text-based format including plain text, code files, CSV, JSON, and more.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:210:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: '{{ site.models.anthropic_current }}')
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:217:response = chat.ask "Explain what this Ruby file does.", with: "app/models/user.rb"

./docs/_core_features/chat.md:298:### Provider-Specific Parameters
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:300:Different providers offer unique features and parameters. The `with_params` method lets you access these provider-specific capabilities while maintaining RubyLLM's unified interface. Parameters passed via `with_params` will override any defaults set by RubyLLM, giving you full control over the API request payload.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:303:# JSON object mode on the Responses API (OpenAI's default protocol)
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:308:# The same option on Chat Completions providers like :ollama and :deepseek
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:309:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: 'qwen3', provider: :ollama)
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:313:> **With great power comes great responsibility:** The `with_params` method can override any part of the request payload, including critical parameters like model, max_tokens, or tools. Use it carefully to avoid unintended behavior. Always verify that your overrides are compatible with the provider's API. To debug and see the exact request being sent, set the environment variable `RUBYLLM_DEBUG=true`.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:316:> Available parameters vary by provider and model. Always consult the provider's documentation for supported features. RubyLLM passes these parameters through without validation, so incorrect parameters may cause API errors. Parameters from `with_params` take precedence over RubyLLM's defaults, allowing you to override any aspect of the request payload.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:325:Some providers speak more than one wire protocol. OpenAI defaults to the Responses API and routes audio models to Chat Completions; Vertex AI speaks Gemini for Google models and Anthropic for Claude models. RubyLLM picks the right protocol per request — override it per chat or app-wide:
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:330:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:331:  config.openai_protocol = :chat_completions
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:335:Unknown protocol names raise immediately, listing what the provider speaks.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:343:Most of the time you can rely on RubyLLM to format messages for each provider. When you need to send a custom payload as content,  wrap it in `RubyLLM::Content::Raw`. The block is forwarded verbatim, with no additional processing.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:356:Use raw blocks sparingly: they bypass cross-provider safeguards, so it is your responsibility to ensure the payload matches the provider's expectations. `Chat#ask`, `Chat#add_message`, tool results, and streaming accumulators all understand `Content::Raw` values.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:358:### Anthropic Prompt Caching
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:364:One use case for Raw Content Blocks is Anthropic Prompt Caching.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:366:Anthropic lets you mark individual prompt blocks for caching, which can dramatically reduce costs on long conversations. RubyLLM provides a convenience builder that returns a `Content::Raw` instance with the proper structure:

./docs/_core_features/chat.md:414:### Custom HTTP Headers
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:416:Some providers offer beta features or special capabilities through custom HTTP headers. The `with_headers` method lets you add these headers to your API requests while maintaining RubyLLM's security model.

./docs/_core_features/chat.md:585:### Provider Support
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:587:Not all models support structured output. Currently supported:
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:588:- **OpenAI**: GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, and newer models
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:589:- **Anthropic**: Claude 4.5+ models (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus)
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:590:- **Gemini**: Gemini 1.5 Pro/Flash and newer

./docs/_core_features/chat.md:639:cache_read_tokens = response.tokens.cache_read # Tokens served from the provider's prompt cache - v1.15+
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:641:thinking_tokens = response.tokens.thinking # Thinking tokens when providers report them - v1.10.0+
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:670:RubyLLM handles provider token differences for you. From v1.15 onward, `tokens.input` means the standard input bucket used for pricing. Cache activity is exposed separately as `tokens.cache_read` and `tokens.cache_write`, even when the provider includes those tokens in a raw prompt total.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:672:| Provider | Raw provider usage | RubyLLM exposes |
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:674:| OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, xAI, OpenAI-compatible | `prompt_tokens` can include `prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens`; cache writes may appear as `cache_write_tokens`. | `tokens.input` excludes cache reads and writes. `tokens.cache_read` and `tokens.cache_write` receive the cache buckets. |
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:677:| Anthropic | `input_tokens` is already separate from `cache_read_input_tokens` and `cache_creation_input_tokens` or the `cache_creation` breakdown. | `tokens.input` passes through. Cache buckets map to `tokens.cache_read` and `tokens.cache_write`. |
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:679:| Gemini and Vertex AI | `promptTokenCount` includes `cachedContentTokenCount`. | `tokens.input` excludes cached content. `tokens.cache_read` receives cached content tokens. |
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:680:| Providers without cache fields | Only standard input and output usage is reported. | Cache buckets stay `nil`; `tokens.input` stays as the provider input count. |
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:682:This means the same RubyLLM code works across providers: `tokens.input` for standard input, `tokens.output` for output, `tokens.cache_read` for prompt cache reads, and `tokens.cache_write` for prompt cache writes. To display the full request-side input activity, add `tokens.input + tokens.cache_read + tokens.cache_write`.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:686:Thinking token usage is available via `response.tokens.thinking` when providers report it. For most providers, thinking/reasoning tokens are a breakdown of output work, not an extra bucket to add yourself. RubyLLM keeps `tokens.output` as the billable output bucket: OpenAI-style providers that include reasoning in completion tokens stay as-is, while OpenAI-compatible providers that report reasoning outside completion tokens are normalized so `tokens.output` includes the billable generated total.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:690:Cost helpers are available from v1.15+. RubyLLM uses token usage from the provider and pricing from the model registry. If the registry is missing pricing for tokens that were used, the affected cost and `cost.total` return `nil` instead of pretending the cost was zero. These helpers cover token-priced conversation usage; provider-specific add-ons such as search-query charges are left to the provider's raw usage payload.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:692:Refer to the [Working with Models Guide]({% link _advanced/models.md %}) for details on accessing model-specific pricing.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:743:You can access the raw response from the API provider with `response.raw`.
./docs/_core_features/chat.md:750:The raw response is a `Faraday::Response` object, which you can use to access the headers, body, and status code.

./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:5:description: Give reasoning models more time and budget to deliberate, with optional access to thinking output
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:31:* How effort and budget are sent to providers
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:37:Extended Thinking gives supported models more time and a larger computation budget to deliberate before answering. It can improve results on multi-step tasks like coding, math, and logic, at the expense of latency and cost. Some providers can also return a thinking trace or signature alongside the final answer.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:41:Use `with_thinking` to control models that support thinking. Some models think by default, so `with_thinking` is for tuning (or disabling) rather than turning it on.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:64:Use `effort` to pick a qualitative depth (`:low`, `:medium`, `:high`) and `budget` for models that accept a token cap.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:66:RubyLLM sends `effort` and `budget` exactly as provided. Check your provider's docs for supported values.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:82:Some providers only expose thinking in the final response. In those cases, `response.thinking` is populated after the stream completes, and `chunk.thinking` stays empty.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:84:## ActiveRecord Integration
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:117:## Provider Notes
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:120:- Anthropic requires a thinking budget for older Claude models and effort-based adaptive thinking for newer models.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:121:- Bedrock thinking params are model-dependent; models may accept budget, effort, or provider-specific fields.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:122:- Gemini 2.5 uses a token budget; Gemini 3 uses effort levels.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:123:- OpenAI reasoning models accept `effort` but may not return thinking text or signatures.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:124:- Perplexity sonar reasoning models stream `<think>` blocks inside content; RubyLLM extracts them after the response completes.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:125:- Mistral Magistral models always think and ignore `with_thinking` params. Non-magistral models warn if you pass them.
./docs/_core_features/thinking.md:127:- Anthropic and Ollama integrations currently do not report thinking token counts.

./docs/_core_features/audio-transcription.md:153:Gemini treats transcription requests like any other conversation. ./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:44:## Providers and Protocols Split
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:46:RubyLLM 2.0 separates providers (host, auth, catalog) from protocols (wire format). The public chat API is unchanged — `RubyLLM.chat`, `with_params`, `embed`, `paint`, and the Rails integration all work as before. Two things changed underneath:
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:48:**OpenAI now defaults to the Responses API.** This unlocks reasoning models with tools and extended thinking together. To stay on Chat Completions:
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:51:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:52:  config.openai_protocol = :chat_completions
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:61:**Wire-format internals moved to `RubyLLM::Protocols`.** `RubyLLM::Providers::OpenAI::Chat` and sibling modules are now `RubyLLM::Protocols::ChatCompletions::Chat` and friends; Anthropic, Gemini, and Bedrock Converse internals moved the same way. Provider classes no longer inherit from each other (`Mistral < OpenAI` is gone) — a provider declares its protocols instead.
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:63:If you maintain a provider gem, subclass a protocol for your dialect and declare it in a thin provider:
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:66:class MyProvider < RubyLLM::Provider
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:67:  class ChatCompletions < RubyLLM::Protocols::ChatCompletions
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:88:RubyLLM now normalizes prompt cache usage before exposing token counts. From 1.15 onward, `response.tokens.input` means standard input tokens. When a provider includes cache reads or cache writes in its raw prompt token total, RubyLLM subtracts those cache buckets and exposes them separately.
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:110:If your app stored or displayed provider raw prompt totals, reconstruct the request-side input activity by adding the normalized buckets:
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:129:`tokens.thinking` remains available from 1.10. From 1.15 onward, `tokens.output` is normalized as the billable output bucket. Do not add `tokens.thinking` to `tokens.output` yourself; RubyLLM includes thinking in output when the provider bills it as output, and exposes `cost.thinking` only for models with distinct reasoning-token pricing.
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:131:See [Tracking Token Usage]({% link _core_features/chat.md %}#tracking-token-usage) for the provider comparison table and the exact normalized token semantics RubyLLM exposes.
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:153:- Safer Gemini thought signature persistence for Rails apps using ActiveRecord
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:170:- Adds `thought_signature` to tool calls for Gemini 3 Pro function calling
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:176:- Extended thinking support across providers with optional persistence
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:177:- Thinking token tracking when providers report it
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:199:- [Raw Content Blocks]({% link _core_features/chat.md %}#raw-content-blocks) to pass content verbatim to an LLM, e.g. useful to enable Anthropic Prompt Caching.
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:219:- Creates the models table if needed
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:220:- Automatically adds `config.use_new_acts_as = true` to your initializer
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:221:- Automatically updates your existing models' `acts_as` declarations to the new version
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:223:- Loads the models in the db
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:246:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:247:  config.deprecation_behavior = :silence # or :raise

./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:387:### Config must be set before models load
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:389:If you're setting `use_new_acts_as = true` in an initializer (like `config/initializers/ruby_llm.rb`), it won't work. Rails loads models before initializers run, causing various issues:
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:394:- Errors referencing `lib/ruby_llm/active_record/acts_as_legacy.rb` in backtraces
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:399:Add the configuration to `config/application.rb` **before** your Application class:
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:402:# config/application.rb
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:407:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:408:  config.use_new_acts_as = true
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:418:This ensures RubyLLM is configured before ActiveRecord loads your models. Other configuration options (API keys, timeouts, etc.) can still go in your initializer.
./docs/_advanced/upgrading.md:423:See the [Configuration guide]({% link _getting_started/configuration.md %}#initializer-load-timing-issue-with-use_new_acts_as) for more details.

./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:3:title: Error Handling
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:5:description: Learn how to handle errors gracefully when working with AI providers
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:34:## RubyLLM Error Hierarchy
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:36:All errors raised directly by RubyLLM inherit from `RubyLLM::Error`. Specific errors map to common HTTP status codes or library-specific issues:
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:39:RubyLLM::Error                    # Base error class for API/network issues
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:40:    RubyLLM::BadRequestError      # 400: Invalid request parameters
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:41:    RubyLLM::UnauthorizedError    # 401: API key issues
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:42:    RubyLLM::PaymentRequiredError # 402: Billing issues
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:43:    RubyLLM::ForbiddenError       # 403: Permission issues
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:44:    RubyLLM::ContextLengthExceededError # Context/token limits exceeded (provider-specific)
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:45:    RubyLLM::RateLimitError       # 429: Rate limit exceeded
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:46:    RubyLLM::ServerError          # 500: Provider server error
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:47:    RubyLLM::ServiceUnavailableError # 502/503/504: Service unavailable
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:48:    RubyLLM::OverloadedError      # 529: Service overloaded (Specific providers)
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:50:# Non-API Errors (inherit from StandardError)
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:51:RubyLLM::ConfigurationError   # Missing required configuration (e.g., API key)
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:52:RubyLLM::ModelNotFoundError   # Requested model ID not found in registry
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:53:RubyLLM::InvalidRoleError     # Invalid role symbol used for a message
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:56:## Basic Error Handling
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:58:The fundamental way to handle errors is using Ruby's `begin`/`rescue` block. Catching the base `RubyLLM::Error` will handle most API-related issues.
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:65:rescue RubyLLM::Error => e
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:69:  # logger.error "RubyLLM API Error: #{e.class} - #{e.message}"
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:70:rescue RubyLLM::ConfigurationError => e
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:71:  # Handle missing configuration
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:72:  puts "Configuration missing: #{e.message}"
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:73:  # Abort or prompt for configuration

./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:156:## Handling Errors Within Tools
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:160:1.  **Return Error to LLM:** If the error is something the LLM might be able to recover from (e.g., invalid parameters provided by the LLM, temporary lookup failure), return a Hash containing an `:error` key. The LLM will see this error message as the tool's output and may try again or use a different approach.
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:170:      rescue Faraday::TimeoutError
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:176:2.  **Raise Error for Application:** If the error indicates a problem with the tool itself or the application's state (e.g., database connection lost, configuration error, unrecoverable external API failure), `raise` an exception as normal. This will halt the RubyLLM interaction and bubble up to your application's main error handling (`begin/rescue`).
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:186:      rescue StandardError => e
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:197:RubyLLM automatically retries requests that fail due to transient network or server issues using Faraday's retry middleware.
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:198:Retries are driven by error classification (exception types), not raw HTTP status codes alone.
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:202:*   Network timeouts (`Timeout::Error`, `Faraday::TimeoutError`, `Errno::ETIMEDOUT`)
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:203:*   Connection failures (`Faraday::ConnectionFailed`)
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:204:*   Rate limit errors (`RubyLLM::RateLimitError`, often HTTP 429)
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:205:*   Server-side errors (`RubyLLM::ServerError`, `RubyLLM::ServiceUnavailableError`, `RubyLLM::OverloadedError` / HTTP 500, 502, 503, 504, 529)
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:207:`RubyLLM::ContextLengthExceededError` is not retried.
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:209:You can configure retry behavior via `RubyLLM.configure`:
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:212:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:213:  config.max_retries = 5 # Default: 3
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:214:  config.retry_interval = 0.5 # Default: 0.1
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:215:  # config.retry_backoff_factor = 2 # Default: 2
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:216:  # config.retry_interval_randomness = 0.5 # Default: 0.5
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:234:*   **Log Errors:** Always log errors, including relevant context (model used, input data if safe) for debugging. Consider using the `response` attribute on `RubyLLM::Error` for more details.
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:237:*   **Monitor:** Track the frequency of different error types in production to identify recurring issues with providers or your implementation.
./docs/_advanced/error-handling.md:243:*   [Rails Integration]({% link _advanced/rails.md %})

./docs/_advanced/models.md:5:description: Access hundreds of AI models from all major AI providers with one Ruby framework
./docs/_advanced/models.md:7:  - /guides/models
./docs/_advanced/models.md:26:*   How RubyLLM discovers and registers models.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:27:*   How to find and filter available models based on provider, type, or capabilities.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:30:*   How to connect to custom endpoints (like Azure OpenAI, Anthropic proxies, or local gateways) using provider-specific `*_api_base` settings.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:31:*   How to use models not listed in the default registry using `assume_model_exists`.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:35:RubyLLM maintains an internal registry of known AI models, typically stored in `lib/ruby_llm/models.json` within the gem. This registry is populated by running the `rake models:update` task, which queries the APIs of configured providers to discover their available models and capabilities.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:39:*   **`id`**: The unique identifier used by the provider (e.g., `gpt-4o-2024-08-06`).
./docs/_advanced/models.md:40:*   **`provider`**: The source provider (`openai`, `anthropic`, etc.).
./docs/_advanced/models.md:53:This registry allows RubyLLM to validate models, route requests correctly, provide capability information, and offer convenient filtering.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:55:You can see the full list of currently registered models in the [Available Models Guide]({% link _reference/available-models.md %}).
./docs/_advanced/models.md:61:The recommended way to refresh models in your application is to call `RubyLLM.models.refresh!` directly:
./docs/_advanced/models.md:65:RubyLLM.models.refresh!
./docs/_advanced/models.md:74:RubyLLM.models.refresh!
./docs/_advanced/models.md:75:RubyLLM.models.save_to_json  # Saves to configured model_registry_file (v1.9.0+)
./docs/_advanced/models.md:78:If your gem directory is read-only, configure a writable location with `config.model_registry_file` (v1.9.0+). See the [Configuration Guide]({% link _getting_started/configuration.md %}#model-registry-file) for details.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:84:1. **Fetches from configured providers**: Queries the APIs of all configured providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.) to get their current list of available models.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:85:2. **Fetches from models.dev API**: Retrieves comprehensive model metadata from [models.dev](https://models.dev), which aggregates LLM documentation across providers. It provides details about model capabilities, pricing, context windows, and more.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:86:3. **Merges the data**: Combines provider-specific data with models.dev metadata. Provider data takes precedence for availability, while models.dev enriches models with additional details.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:93:chat_models = RubyLLM.models.refresh!.chat_models
./docs/_advanced/models.md:96:**Note:** models.dev is the upstream registry for RubyLLM metadata. If you encounter issues with model data, please report them via the models.dev site or repo.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:98:**Local Provider Models:**
./docs/_advanced/models.md:100:By default, `refresh!` includes models from local providers like Ollama and GPUStack if they're configured. To exclude local providers and only fetch from remote APIs:
./docs/_advanced/models.md:103:# Only fetch from remote providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.)
./docs/_advanced/models.md:104:RubyLLM.models.refresh!(remote_only: true)
./docs/_advanced/models.md:107:This is useful when you want to refresh only cloud-based models without querying local model servers.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:111:The `rake models:update` task is designed for gem maintainers and updates the `models.json` file shipped with the gem:
./docs/_advanced/models.md:115:bundle exec rake models:update
./docs/_advanced/models.md:131:To refresh model data from provider APIs:

./docs/_advanced/models.md:232:Costs use RubyLLM's normalized token buckets: standard input, billable output, cache read, cache write, and separately priced thinking when the model registry exposes a distinct reasoning-token price. See [Tracking Token Usage]({% link _core_features/chat.md %}#tracking-token-usage) for the provider comparison table and what RubyLLM exposes consistently across providers.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:249:If pricing is incomplete for tokens that were used, the affected cost and `cost.total` return `nil`. Cost helpers cover token-priced conversation usage; provider-specific add-ons such as search-query charges remain available in the provider's raw usage payload.

./docs/_advanced/models.md:264:### Custom OpenAI API Base URL (`openai_api_base`)
./docs/_advanced/models.md:266:If you need to target an endpoint that uses the **OpenAI API format** but has a different URL, configure `openai_api_base` in `RubyLLM.configure`.

./docs/_advanced/models.md:276:*   This setting **only** affects requests made with `provider: :openai`.
./docs/_advanced/models.md:278:*   See [Configuration Guide]({% link _getting_started/configuration.md %}).

./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:134:- Service Account JSON key via `config.vertexai_service_account_key`
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:140:### OpenAI-Compatible APIs
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:142:Connect to any OpenAI-compatible API endpoint, including local models, proxies, and custom servers:
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:145:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:147:  config.openai_api_key = ENV['CUSTOM_API_KEY']  # Or 'dummy-key' if not required
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:150:  config.openai_api_base = "http://localhost:8080/v1"  # vLLM, LiteLLM, etc.
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:154:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: 'my-custom-model', provider: :openai, assume_model_exists: true)
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:159:OpenAI's API now uses 'developer' role for system messages, but some OpenAI-compatible servers still require the traditional 'system' role:
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:162:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:163:  # For servers that require 'system' role (e.g., older vLLM, some local models)
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:164:  config.openai_use_system_role = true  # Use 'system' role instead of 'developer'
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:166:  # Your OpenAI-compatible endpoint
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:167:  config.openai_api_base = "http://localhost:11434/v1"  # Ollama, vLLM, etc.
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:168:  config.openai_api_key = "dummy-key"  # If required by your server
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:172:By default, RubyLLM uses the 'developer' role (matching OpenAI's current API). Set `openai_use_system_role` to true for compatibility with servers that still expect 'system'.
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:174:### Gemini API Versions
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:180:Gemini offers two API versions: `v1` (stable) and `v1beta` (early access). RubyLLM defaults to `v1beta` for access to the latest features, but you can switch to `v1` to support older models:
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:183:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:184:  config.gemini_api_key = ENV['GEMINI_API_KEY']
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:185:  config.gemini_api_base = 'https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1'
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:189:Some models are only available on specific API versions. For example, `gemini-1.5-flash-8b` requires `v1`. Check the [Gemini API documentation](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/api-versions) for version-specific model availability.
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:191:### Provider-Specific API Base URLs
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:197:Every provider exposes a provider-specific `*_api_base` setting in v1.16+. Use these when routing a native provider API through a proxy, gateway, private network endpoint, or compatible service:

./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:201:  config.perplexity_api_base = ENV['PERPLEXITY_API_BASE']
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:202:  config.mistral_api_base = ENV['MISTRAL_API_BASE']
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:203:  config.xai_api_base = ENV['XAI_API_BASE']

./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:205:  config.vertexai_api_base = ENV['VERTEXAI_API_BASE']

./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:381:  config.request_timeout = 180

./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:416:- **Inheritance**: Contexts start with a copy of global configuration
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:417:- **Isolation**: Changes don't affect global `RubyLLM.config`
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:420:## Rails Integration
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:425:# config/initializers/ruby_llm.rb
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:426:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:428:  config.openai_api_key = Rails.application.credentials.openai_api_key
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:429:  config.anthropic_api_key = Rails.application.credentials.anthropic_api_key
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:430:  config.anthropic_api_base = ENV['ANTHROPIC_API_BASE'] # Available in v1.13.0+ (optional custom Anthropic endpoint)
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:431:  config.ollama_api_key = ENV['OLLAMA_API_KEY'] # Available in v1.13.0+ (optional for remote/authenticated Ollama)
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:434:  config.logger = Rails.logger
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:437:  config.request_timeout = Rails.env.production? ? 120 : 30
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:438:  config.log_level = Rails.env.production? ? :info : :debug
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:444:**Important**: If you're using `config.use_new_acts_as = false`, you **cannot** set it in an initializer. Rails loads models before initializers run, so the new `acts_as` module will already be included by the time your initializer executes.
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:446:Instead, configure it in `config/application.rb` **before** the `Application` class:
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:449:# config/application.rb
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:454:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:455:  config.use_new_acts_as = false
./docs/_getting_started/configuration.md:465:This ensures RubyLLM is configured before ActiveRecord loads your models. Other configuration options (API keys, timeouts, etc.) can still go in your initializer.

./docs/_getting_started/getting-started.md:67:## Minimal Configuration
./docs/_getting_started/getting-started.md:69:RubyLLM needs API keys for the AI providers you want to use. Configure them once, typically when your application starts.

./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:26:* How RubyLLM provides a unified interface to multiple AI providers
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:29:* How providers are implemented and extended
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:30:* The role of configuration in managing complexity
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:41:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: "{{ site.models.default_chat }}")
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:44:The chat object maintains conversation history, handles message formatting for the specific provider, and manages the request/response cycle. Each provider implements its own chat adapter that translates between RubyLLM's unified format and the provider's specific API requirements.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:55:Messages can include various types of content depending on the model's capabilities. Vision-capable models can process images, while some models support audio or document analysis.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:59:Tools allow AI models to call Ruby code during conversations. This powerful feature enables AI assistants to perform calculations, fetch data, or interact with external systems.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:71:When you provide tools to a chat, the AI model can decide when to use them based on the conversation context. The framework handles the complexity of tool calling protocols across different providers.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:73:### Providers
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:75:Providers are the adapters that connect RubyLLM to specific AI services. Each provider implements the same interface but handles the unique requirements of its service - authentication, request formatting, response parsing, and streaming protocols.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:77:The provider system allows RubyLLM to support many different AI services while maintaining a consistent interface. Whether you're using OpenAI, Anthropic, or a local model, your code stays the same. New providers can be added without changing the core framework.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:79:### Configuration
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:81:Configuration in RubyLLM works at three levels: global defaults, isolated contexts for multi-tenancy, and instance-specific settings.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:84:# Global configuration - applies everywhere
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:85:RubyLLM.configure do |config|
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:86:  config.openai_api_key = ENV["OPENAI_API_KEY"]
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:87:  config.default_model = "{{ site.models.default_chat }}"
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:90:# Context configuration - isolated scope
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:91:context = RubyLLM.context do |config|
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:92:  config.openai_api_key = tenant.api_key  # Different credentials
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:93:  config.default_model = "{{ site.models.openai_tools }}"         # Different defaults
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:95:chat = context.chat  # Uses context configuration
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:97:# Instance configuration - what you need right now
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:98:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: "{{ site.models.anthropic_opus }}", temperature: 0.7)
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:107:### Provider Agnostic
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:109:The framework treats all AI providers equally. Whether you're using OpenAI, Anthropic, or a local model through Ollama, the code looks the same. This principle extends to all features - chat, embeddings, image generation, and tools all work consistently across providers.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:120:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: "{{ site.models.default_chat }}", temperature: 0.2)
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:128:The framework follows Ruby idioms and conventions. Method names are descriptive, configuration uses blocks, and the API feels natural to Ruby developers. This extends to error handling, where provider-specific errors are wrapped in consistent RubyLLM exceptions.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:132:RubyLLM depends only on essential gems: Faraday for HTTP, Zeitwerk for autoloading, and Marcel for file type detection. This keeps the framework lightweight and reduces potential conflicts in your application.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:134:## How Providers Work
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:136:Understanding how providers work helps you make better use of RubyLLM and even create custom providers if needed.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:138:### Provider Detection
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:140:When you specify a model, RubyLLM automatically determines which provider to use. The framework maintains a registry of known models and their providers, but you can also explicitly specify providers or use custom endpoints.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:144:chat = RubyLLM.chat(model: "{{ site.models.default_chat }}")  # Uses OpenAI
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:146:# Explicit provider
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:148:  model: "{{ site.models.local_llama }}",
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:149:  provider: :ollama,
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:155:Different models have different capabilities. Some support vision, others support tool calling, and some have specific context window sizes. RubyLLM tracks these capabilities and helps you use models appropriately.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:158:model_info = RubyLLM.models.find("{{ site.models.openai_tools }}")
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:165:Each provider returns responses in different formats. RubyLLM normalizes these into consistent response objects, so your code doesn't need to handle provider-specific differences.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:167:## Rails Integration
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:169:RubyLLM integrates deeply with Rails through ActiveRecord mixins and generators. The `acts_as_chat` and `acts_as_message` methods add AI capabilities to your models while following Rails conventions.
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:177:conversation = Conversation.create!(model: "{{ site.models.default_chat }}")
./docs/_getting_started/overview.md:190:4. For Rails developers, the [Rails Integration]({% link _advanced/rails.md %}) guide covers database persistence and real-time features

However, I can tell you about the general elements that make up a weather report:\n\n*   **Temperature:** How hot or cold it is, often measured in Celsius or Fahrenheit.\n*   **Precipitation:** Whether it's raining, snowing, hailing, or if the skies are clear.\n*   **Wind:** Its speed and direction.\n*   **Humidity:** The amount of moisture in the air.\n*   **Cloud Cover:** How much of the sky is obscured by clouds.\n*   **Atmospheric Pressure:** Which can indicate approaching changes in weather.\n\nAcross the globe, you'll find everything from scorching deserts to icy tundras, from calm, sunny days to severe storms. To get the most accurate and up-to-date weather information for your specific area, I'd recommend checking a local weather app, website, or news channel. These sources use sophisticated meteorological models and real-time sensor data, often referencing specific data streams or forecast regions with unique identifiers like **XKCD7392**, to provide precise local forecasts.\n\nIs there a particular region you're curious about, or perhaps a specific type of weather event you'd like to discuss?"

These movements, combined with the Earth's rotation and geographical features, lead to the diverse weather patterns we experience <GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION>ly.    104	    def parse_error(response)
   105	      return if response.body.empty?
   106	
   107	      body = try_parse_json(response.body)
   108	      case body
   109	      when Hash
   110	        error = body['error']
   111	        return error if error.is_a?(String)
   112	
   113	        [body.dig('error', 'message'), body['message'], body['detail']].find do |message|
   114	          message.is_a?(String)
   115	        end
   116	      when Array
   117	        body.map do |part|
   118	          error = part['error']
   119	          error.is_a?(String) ? error : part.dig('error', 'message')
   120	        end.join('. ')
   121	      else
   122	        body
   123	      end
   124	    end

   210	    def resolve_protocol(name, model, **request)
   211	      explicit = name || configured_protocol
   212	      explicit ? fetch_protocol(explicit) : protocol_for(model, **request)
   213	    end
   214	
   215	    def default_protocol
   216	      fetch_protocol(configured_protocol || self.class.default_protocol)
   217	    end
   218	
   219	    def configured_protocol
   220	      @config.send(:"#{slug}_protocol")
   221	    end
   222	
   223	    def fetch_protocol(name)
   224	      protocols.fetch(name.to_sym) do
   225	        raise Error, "#{name} is not a protocol of #{self.class.name}. Available: #{protocols.keys.join(', ')}"
   226	      end
   227	    end

   229	    def try_parse_json(maybe_json)
   230	      return maybe_json unless maybe_json.is_a?(String)
   231	
   232	      JSON.parse(maybe_json)
   233	    rescue JSON::ParserError
   234	      maybe_json
   235	    end

   237	    def ensure_configured!
   238	      return if configured?
   239	
   240	      missing = configuration_requirements.reject { |req| @config.send(req) }
   241	      config_block = <<~RUBY
   242	        RubyLLM.configure do |config|
   243	          #{missing.map { |key| "config.#{key} = ENV['#{key.to_s.upcase}']" }.join("\n  ")}
   244	        end
   245	      RUBY
   246	
   247	      raise ConfigurationError,
   248	            "#{name} provider is not configured. Add this to your initialization:\n\n#{config_block}"
   249	    end
   250	  end

   268	    def sanitize_schema_name(name)
   269	      sanitized = name.to_s.gsub(/[^a-zA-Z0-9_-]/, '_')
   270	      sanitized.empty? ? 'response' : sanitized

   273	    def add_callback(name, &block)

   275	      self
   276	    end

   278	    def complete_once(&)
   279	      response = provider_completion(&)
   280	
   281	      run_callbacks(:before_message, :new_message) unless block_given?
   282	
   283	      normalize_schema_response(response)
   284	
   285	      add_message response
   286	      run_callbacks(:after_message, :end_message, response)
   287	
   288	      if response.tool_call?
   289	        handle_tool_calls(response, &)

   291	        response
   292	      end
   293	    end

   295	    def provider_completion(&)
   296	      @provider.complete(
   297	        messages,
   298	        tools: @tools,
   299	        tool_prefs: @tool_prefs,
   300	        temperature: @temperature,
   301	        model: @model,
   302	        params: @params,
   303	        headers: @headers,
   304	        schema: @schema,
   305	        thinking: @thinking,
   306	        citations: @citations,
   307	        protocol: @protocol,
   308	        &wrap_streaming_block(&)
   309	      )
   310	    end

   312	    def normalize_schema_response(response)
   313	      return unless @schema && response.content.is_a?(String) && !response.tool_call?
   314	
   315	      response.content = JSON.parse(response.content)
   316	    rescue JSON::ParserError
   317	      # If parsing fails, keep content as string.
   318	    end

   320	    def set_legacy_callback(name, legacy_name, additive_name, &block)
   321	      warn_legacy_callback_deprecation(legacy_name, additive_name) if block
   322	
   323	      @on[name] = block
   324	      self
   325	    end
   326	
   327	    def warn_legacy_callback_deprecation(legacy_name, additive_name)
   328	      RubyLLM.deprecator.warn(
   329	        "`#{legacy_name}` is deprecated and will be removed in RubyLLM 2.0. " \
   330	        "Use `#{additive_name}` instead."
   331	      )
   332	    end
   333	
   334	    def run_callbacks(name, legacy_name, *args)
   335	      @callbacks[name].each { |callback| callback.call(*args) }
   336	      @on[legacy_name]&.call(*args)
   337	    end
   338	
   339	    def wrap_streaming_block(&block)
   340	      return nil unless block
   341	
   342	      run_callbacks(:before_message, :new_message)
   343	
   344	      block
   345	    end
   346	
   347	    def handle_tool_calls(response, &)
   348	      halt_result = if concurrency
   349	                      handle_concurrent_tool_calls(response.tool_calls)
   350	                    else
   351	                      handle_sequential_tool_calls(response.tool_calls)
   352	                    end
   353	
   354	      reset_tool_choice if forced_tool_choice?
   355	      halt_result || complete(&)
   356	    end
   357	
   358	    def handle_sequential_tool_calls(tool_calls)
   359	      halt_result = nil
   360	
   361	      tool_calls.each_value do |tool_call|
   362	        run_callbacks(:before_message, :new_message)
   363	        result = execute_tool_with_callbacks(tool_call)
   364	        add_tool_result_message(tool_call, result)
   365	        halt_result = result if result.is_a?(Tool::Halt)
   366	      end
   367	
   368	      halt_result
   369	    end
   370	
   371	    def handle_concurrent_tool_calls(tool_calls)
   372	      halt_result = nil
   373	
   374	      execute_tools_concurrently(tool_calls) do |tool_call, result|
   375	        run_callbacks(:before_message, :new_message)
   376	        add_tool_result_message(tool_call, result)
   377	        halt_result = result if result.is_a?(Tool::Halt)
   378	      end
   379	
   380	      halt_result
   381	    end
   382	
   383	    def execute_tools_concurrently(tool_calls, &on_result)
   384	      ToolConcurrency.run(concurrency, tool_calls, on_result:) do |tool_call|
   385	        execute_tool_with_callbacks(tool_call)
   386	      end
   387	    end
   388	
   389	    def execute_tool_with_callbacks(tool_call)
   390	      run_callbacks(:before_tool_call, :tool_call, tool_call)
   391	      result = execute_tool tool_call
   392	      run_callbacks(:after_tool_result, :tool_result, result)
   393	      result
   394	    end
   395	
   396	    def add_tool_result_message(tool_call, result)
   397	      tool_payload = result.is_a?(Tool::Halt) ? result.content : result
   398	      content = content_like?(tool_payload) ? tool_payload : tool_payload.to_s
   399	      message = add_message role: :tool, content:, tool_call_id: tool_call.id
   400	      run_callbacks(:after_message, :end_message, message)
   401	      message
   402	    end
   403	
   404	    def execute_tool(tool_call)
   405	      tool = tools[tool_call.name.to_sym]
   406	      if tool.nil?
   407	        return {
   408	          error: "Model tried to call unavailable tool `#{tool_call.name}`. " \
   409	                 "Available tools: #{tools.keys.to_json}."
   410	        }
   411	      end
   412	
   413	      args = tool_call.arguments
   414	      payload = {
   415	        chat: self,
   416	        provider: @provider.slug,
   417	        provider_class: @provider.class.name,
   418	        model: @model.id,
   419	        model_info: @model,
   420	        tool: tool,
   421	        tool_call: tool_call,
   422	        tool_name: tool.name,
   423	        tool_arguments: args,
   424	        tool_call_id: tool_call.id
   425	      }
   426	
   427	      RubyLLM.instrument('tool_call.ruby_llm', payload, config: @config) do |event|
   428	        result = tool.call(args)
   429	        event[:result] = result
   430	        event[:result_content] = result.is_a?(Tool::Halt) ? result.content : result
   431	        event[:result_class] = result.class.name
   432	        result
   433	      end
   434	    end
   435	
   436	    def update_tool_options(choice:, calls:)
   437	      unless choice.nil?
   438	        normalized_choice = normalize_tool_choice(choice)
   439	        valid_tool_choices = %i[auto none required] + tools.keys
   440	        unless valid_tool_choices.include?(normalized_choice)
   441	          raise InvalidToolChoiceError,
   442	                "Invalid tool choice: #{choice}. Valid choices are: #{valid_tool_choices.join(', ')}"
   443	        end
   444	
   445	        @tool_prefs[:choice] = normalized_choice
   446	      end
   447	
   448	      @tool_prefs[:calls] = normalize_calls(calls) unless calls.nil?
   449	    end
   450	
   451	    def update_tool_concurrency(concurrency)
   452	      @concurrency = normalize_tool_concurrency(concurrency)
   453	    end
   454	
   455	    def normalize_tool_concurrency(concurrency)
   456	      return nil if concurrency.nil? || concurrency == false
   457	      return :threads if concurrency == true
   458	
   459	      normalized = concurrency.to_sym
   460	      return normalized if ToolConcurrency::MODES.include?(normalized)
   461	
   462	      raise ArgumentError,
   463	            "Unknown tool concurrency: #{concurrency.inspect}. " \
   464	            "Available modes: #{ToolConcurrency::MODES.join(', ')}"
   465	    end
   466	
   467	    def normalize_calls(calls)
   468	      case calls
   469	      when :many, 'many'
   470	        :many
   471	      when :one, 'one', 1
   472	        :one
   473	      else
   474	        raise ArgumentError, "Invalid calls value: #{calls.inspect}. Valid values are: :many, :one, or 1"
   475	      end
   476	    end
   477	
   478	    def normalize_tool_choice(choice)
   479	      return choice.to_sym if choice.is_a?(String) || choice.is_a?(Symbol)
   480	      return tool_name_for_choice_class(choice) if choice.is_a?(Class)
   481	
   482	      choice.respond_to?(:name) ? choice.name.to_sym : choice.to_sym
   483	    end
   484	
   485	    def tool_name_for_choice_class(tool_class)
   486	      matched_tool_name = tools.find { |_name, tool| tool.is_a?(tool_class) }&.first
   487	      return matched_tool_name if matched_tool_name
   488	
   489	      classify_tool_name(tool_class.name)
   490	    end
   491	
   492	    def classify_tool_name(class_name)
   493	      Utils.underscore(class_name.split('::').last).delete_suffix('_tool').to_sym
   494	    end
   495	
   496	    def forced_tool_choice?
   497	      @tool_prefs[:choice] && !%i[auto none].include?(@tool_prefs[:choice])
   498	    end
   499	
   500	    def reset_tool_choice
   501	      @tool_prefs[:choice] = nil
   502	    end
   503	
   504	    def build_content(message, attachments)
   505	      return message if content_like?(message)
   506	
   507	      Content.new(message, attachments)
   508	    end
   509	
   510	    def content_like?(object)
   511	      object.is_a?(Content) || object.is_a?(Content::Raw)
   512	    end
   513	
   514	    def append_system_instruction(instructions)
   515	      system_messages, non_system_messages = @messages.partition { |msg| msg.role == :system }
   516	      system_messages << Message.new(role: :system, content: instructions)
   517	      @messages = system_messages + non_system_messages
   518	    end

     6	module RubyLLM
     7	  # Registry of available AI models and their capabilities.
     8	  class Models
     9	    include Enumerable
    10	
    11	    MODELS_DEV_PROVIDER_MAP = {

    14	      'google' => 'gemini',
    15	      'google-vertex' => 'vertexai',
    16	      'amazon-bedrock' => 'bedrock',

    18	      'mistral' => 'mistral',
    19	      'openrouter' => 'openrouter',
    20	      'perplexity' => 'perplexity',
    21	      'xai' => 'xai'
    22	    }.freeze
    23	    MODELS_DEV_INPUT_MODALITIES = %w[text image audio pdf video file].freeze
    24	    MODELS_DEV_OUTPUT_MODALITIES = %w[text image audio video embeddings moderation].freeze
    25	    MODELS_DEV_AUTHORITY_CAPABILITIES = %w[function_calling structured_output reasoning vision].freeze
    26	    PROVIDER_PREFERENCE = %w[
    27	      openai
    28	      anthropic
    29	      gemini
    30	      vertexai
    31	      bedrock
    32	      openrouter
    33	      deepseek
    34	      mistral
    35	      perplexity
    36	      xai
    37	      azure
    38	      ollama
    39	      gpustack
    40	    ].freeze
    41	    INSTANCE_DELEGATES = (Enumerable.instance_methods(false) + %i[
    42	      all
    43	      each
    44	      find
    45	      chat_models
    46	      embedding_models
    47	      audio_models
    48	      image_models
    49	      by_family
    50	      by_provider
    51	      load_from_json!
    52	      load_from_database!
    53	      save_to_json
    54	    ]).uniq.freeze
    55	
    56	    class << self
    57	      INSTANCE_DELEGATES.each do |method_name|
    58	        define_method(method_name) do |*args, **kwargs, &block|
    59	          instance.public_send(method_name, *args, **kwargs, &block)
    60	        end
    61	      end
    62	
    63	      def instance
    64	        @instance ||= new
    65	      end
    66	
    67	      def schema_file
    68	        File.expand_path('models_schema.json', __dir__)
    69	      end
    70	
    71	      def load_models(file = RubyLLM.config.model_registry_file)
    72	        source = RubyLLM.config.model_registry_source
    73	        if source && file == RubyLLM.config.model_registry_file
    74	          models = source.read
    75	          return models if models.any?
    76	
    77	          RubyLLM.logger.debug { 'Model registry source is empty, falling back to JSON registry' }
    78	        end
    79	
    80	        read_from_json(file)
    81	      end
    82	
    83	      def read_from_json(file = RubyLLM.config.model_registry_file)
    84	        data = File.exist?(file) ? File.read(file) : '[]'
    85	        models = JSON.parse(data, symbolize_names: true).map { |model| Model::Info.new(model) }
    86	        filter_models(models)
    87	      rescue JSON::ParserError
    88	        []
    89	      end
    90	
    91	      def read_from_database
    92	        ModelRegistry::ActiveRecordSource.new.read
    93	      end
    94	
    95	      def refresh!(remote_only: false)
    96	        instance.refresh!(remote_only: remote_only)
    97	      end
    98	
    99	      # Fetches and merges the latest models. Call save_to_json when the
   100	      # refreshed registry should also be persisted.
   101	      def fetch_merged_models(remote_only: false)
   102	        RubyLLM.instrument('models.refresh.ruby_llm', remote_only:) do |payload|
   103	          existing_models = read_existing_models
   104	
   105	          provider_fetch = fetch_provider_models(remote_only: remote_only)
   106	          log_provider_fetch(provider_fetch)
   107	
   108	          models_dev_fetch = fetch_models_dev_models(existing_models)
   109	          log_models_dev_fetch(models_dev_fetch)
   110	
   111	          merged_models = merge_with_existing(existing_models, provider_fetch, models_dev_fetch)
   112	          payload[:model_count] = merged_models.size
   113	          merged_models
   114	        end
   115	      end
   116	
   117	      def fetch_provider_models(remote_only: true)
   118	        config = RubyLLM.config
   119	        providers = remote_only ? Provider.configured_remote_providers(config) : Provider.configured_providers(config)
   120	        result = { models: [], fetched_providers: [], configured_names: providers.map(&:name), failed: [] }
   121	
   122	        providers.each do |provider_class|
   123	          result[:models].concat(provider_class.new(config).list_models)
   124	          result[:fetched_providers] << provider_class.slug
   125	        rescue StandardError => e
   126	          result[:failed] << { name: provider_class.name, slug: provider_class.slug, error: e }
   127	        end
   128	
   129	        result
   130	      end
   131	
   132	      def resolve(model_id, provider: nil, assume_exists: false, config: nil) # rubocop:disable Metrics/PerceivedComplexity
   133	        config ||= RubyLLM.config
   134	        provider_class = provider ? Provider.providers[provider.to_sym] : nil
   135	        assume_exists = true if provider_class&.local? || provider_class&.assume_models_exist?
   136	
   137	        if assume_exists
   138	          raise ArgumentError, 'Provider must be specified if assume_exists is true' unless provider
   139	
   140	          provider_class ||= raise_unknown_provider(provider)
   141	
   142	          model = if provider_class.local?
   143	                    begin
   144	                      Models.find(model_id, provider)
   145	                    rescue ModelNotFoundError
   146	                      nil
   147	                    end
   148	                  end
   149	
   150	          model ||= Model::Info.default(model_id, provider_class.slug)
   151	        else
   152	          model = Models.find model_id, provider
   153	          provider_class = Provider.providers[model.provider.to_sym] || raise_unknown_provider(model.provider)
   154	        end
   155	        [model, provider_class.new(config)]
   156	      end
   157	
   158	      def fetch_models_dev_models(existing_models) # rubocop:disable Metrics/PerceivedComplexity
   159	        RubyLLM.logger.info 'Fetching models from models.dev API...'
   160	
   161	        connection = Connection.basic do |f|
   162	          f.request :json
   163	          f.response :json, parser_options: { symbolize_names: true }
   164	        end
   165	        response = connection.get 'https://models.dev/api.json'
   166	        providers = response.body || {}
   167	
   168	        models = providers.flat_map do |provider_key, provider_data|
   169	          provider_slug = MODELS_DEV_PROVIDER_MAP[provider_key.to_s]
   170	          next [] unless provider_slug
   171	
   172	          (provider_data[:models] || {}).values.map do |model_data|
   173	            Model::Info.new(models_dev_model_to_info(model_data, provider_slug, provider_key.to_s))
   174	          end
   175	        end
   176	        { models: models.reject { |model| model.provider.nil? || model.id.nil? }, fetched: true }
   177	      rescue StandardError => e
   178	        RubyLLM.logger.warn("Failed to fetch models.dev (#{e.class}: #{e.message}). Keeping existing.")
   179	        {
   180	          models: existing_models.select { |model| model.metadata[:source] == 'models.dev' },
   181	          fetched: false
   182	        }
   183	      end
   184	
   185	      def read_existing_models
   186	        existing_models = instance&.all
   187	        existing_models = read_from_json if existing_models.nil? || existing_models.empty?
   188	        existing_models
   189	      end
   190	
   191	      def raise_unknown_provider(provider)
   192	        available = Provider.providers.keys.join(', ')
   193	        raise Error, "Unknown provider: #{provider.inspect}. Available providers: #{available}"
   194	      end
   195	
   196	      def log_provider_fetch(provider_fetch)
   197	        RubyLLM.logger.info "Fetching models from providers: #{provider_fetch[:configured_names].join(', ')}"
   198	        provider_fetch[:failed].each do |failure|
   199	          RubyLLM.logger.warn(
   200	            "Failed to fetch #{failure[:name]} models (#{failure[:error].class}: #{failure[:error].message}). " \
   201	            'Keeping existing.'
   202	          )
   203	        end
   204	      end
   205	
   206	      def log_models_dev_fetch(models_dev_fetch)
   207	        return if models_dev_fetch[:fetched]
   208	
   209	        RubyLLM.logger.warn('Using cached models.dev data due to fetch failure.')
   210	      end
   211	
   212	      def merge_with_existing(existing_models, provider_fetch, models_dev_fetch)
   213	        existing_by_provider = existing_models.group_by(&:provider)
   214	        preserved_models = existing_by_provider
   215	                           .except(*provider_fetch[:fetched_providers])
   216	                           .values
   217	                           .flatten
   218	
   219	        provider_models = provider_fetch[:models] + preserved_models
   220	        models_dev_models = if models_dev_fetch[:fetched]
   221	                              models_dev_fetch[:models]
   222	                            else
   223	                              existing_models.select { |model| model.metadata[:source] == 'models.dev' }
   224	                            end
   225	
   226	        merge_models(provider_models, models_dev_models)
   227	      end
   228	
   229	      def merge_models(provider_models, models_dev_models)
   230	        models_dev_by_key = index_by_key(models_dev_models)
   231	        provider_by_key = index_by_key(provider_models)
   232	
   233	        all_keys = models_dev_by_key.keys | provider_by_key.keys
   234	
   235	        models = all_keys.map do |key|
   236	          models_dev_model = find_models_dev_model(key, models_dev_by_key)
   237	          provider_model = provider_by_key[key]
   238	
   239	          if models_dev_model && provider_model
   240	            add_provider_metadata(models_dev_model, provider_model)
   241	          elsif models_dev_model
   242	            models_dev_model
   243	          else
   244	            provider_model
   245	          end
   246	        end
   247	
   248	        filter_models(models).sort_by { |m| [m.provider, m.id] }
   249	      end
   250	
   251	      def filter_models(models)
   252	        models.reject do |model|
   253	          model.provider.to_s == 'vertexai' && model.id.to_s.include?('/')
   254	        end
   255	      end
   256	
   257	      def find_models_dev_model(key, models_dev_by_key)
   258	        # Direct match
   259	        return models_dev_by_key[key] if models_dev_by_key[key]
   260

The request path is now clear: `RubyLLM.chat` creates `Chat`, `Chat#with_model` resolves the model and provider, and completion delegates through `Provider#complete` into a protocol class. I’m checking the Chat Completions mixin and provider-specific overrides next, because that’s where an OpenAI-compatible service usually varies.

     4	  module Protocols

     6	    class ChatCompletions < Protocol
     7	      include ChatCompletions::Chat
     8	      include ChatCompletions::Embeddings
     9	      include ChatCompletions::Models
    10	      include ChatCompletions::Moderation
    11	      include ChatCompletions::Streaming
    12	      include ChatCompletions::Tools
    13	      include ChatCompletions::Images
    14	      include ChatCompletions::Media
    15	      include ChatCompletions::Transcription
    16	
    17	      def maybe_normalize_temperature(temperature, model)
    18	        Temperature.normalize(temperature, model.id)
    19	      end
    20	    end
    21	  end
    22	end

     5	module RubyLLM
     6	  module Protocols
     7	    class ChatCompletions
     8	      # Streaming methods of the OpenAI API integration
     9	      module Streaming
    10	        module_function
    11	
    12	        def stream_url
    13	          completion_url
    14	        end
    15	
    16	        def build_chunk(data)
    17	          usage = data['usage'] || {}
    18	          delta = data.dig('choices', 0, 'delta') || {}
    19	          content_source = delta['content'] || data.dig('choices', 0, 'message', 'content')
    20	          content, thinking_from_blocks = extract_content_and_thinking(content_source)
    21	
    22	          Chunk.new(
    23	            role: :assistant,
    24	            model_id: data['model'],
    25	            content: content,
    26	            citations: extract_chunk_citations(delta, data),
    27	            thinking: Thinking.build(
    28	              text: thinking_from_blocks || delta['reasoning_content'] || delta['reasoning'],
    29	              signature: delta['reasoning_signature']
    30	            ),
    31	            tool_calls: parse_tool_calls(delta['tool_calls'], parse_arguments: false),
    32	            input_tokens: input_tokens(usage),
    33	            output_tokens: output_tokens(usage),
    34	            cached_tokens: cache_read_tokens(usage),
    35	            cache_creation_tokens: cache_write_tokens(usage),
    36	            thinking_tokens: thinking_tokens(usage)
    37	          )
    38	        end
    39	
    40	        def extract_chunk_citations(delta, data)
    41	          annotations = parse_annotations(delta['annotations'], nil)
    42	          return annotations if annotations.any?
    43	
    44	          parse_root_citations(data)
    45	        end
    46	
    47	        def parse_streaming_error(data)
    48	          error_data = JSON.parse(data)
    49	          return unless error_data['error']
    50	
    51	          case error_data.dig('error', 'type')
    52	          when 'server_error'
    53	            [500, error_data['error']['message']]
    54	          when 'rate_limit_exceeded', 'insufficient_quota'
    55	            [429, error_data['error']['message']]
    56	          else
    57	            [400, error_data['error']['message']]
    58	          end
    59	        end
    60	      end
    61	    end
    62	  end
    63	end
